segments.text: Welcome everyone. I'm Anand Kulkarni, CEO
and founder of Core Story. Here's the
question that every engineering team
faces. Your static analysis tool is
doing a great job. It's finding
thousands of issues in your code base.
Bugs, vulnerabilities, code smells,
security hotspots.
But which issues actually need your
organization's attention right now? And
which ones are just noise? That's the
problem that we're going to show you how
to solve today. I'm going to show you
how Core Story and Sonar work
together to turn raw findings into
actionable impactbased decisions. Here's
the root of the issue. Sonar does a
great job surfacing every issue. That's
exactly what we want from our quality
platform. But severity of issues and
business impact of issues are two
different things. Take a look at these
three findings. We have two blockers and
a medium. The blockers are in a text
fixture and a date formatter.
Intentional patterns that are actually
correct code. The medium is an injection
vector in your payment module. That's
the one that needs immediate attention
from your engineering team. Without
architectural context, teams can't tell
the difference at a glance. As a result,
we end up ignoring findings,
accumulating security debt, or wasting
time investigating noise. This is a
problem known as the triage gap. The
difference between what our systems can
surface for us and what we understand to
be truly essential for ameliating real
issues.
Here's how the solution works. Both
Sonar and Core Story expose MCP
servers, the model context protocol.
That means that any MCP compatible AI
agent can talk to both systems
simultaneously.
The agent queries sonar for
findings, severity, and quality gate
status. It also queries core story for
architectural context, what each
component does, who depends on it, and
what the design intent of that feature
or behavior or line of code was. Then it
synthesizes both of these into
actionable decisions. There's no custom
integration and no middleware to
maintain. Both servers are free and easy
to set up.
This creates a continuous loop.
Step one, investigate. Sonar
surfaces findings with severity
rankings. Core stories knowledge graph
explains what the affected code actually
does. Is it a production API surface? A
test fixture, who depends on it?
Step two, triage. Core Story's
architectural context lets the agent
make real prioritization decisions at
machine speed. Real issues get escalated
with blast radius analysis, telling us
what is going to be affected by making a
change. Intentional patterns get
documented and closed. Step three is
build. When it's time to fix something
or write new code, core story provides
design patterns from your codebase, the
agent writes code that follows those
patterns to resolve the issue. Sonar
validates that against quality
rules. That means you get clean code on
your first try. This closed loop system
and closed loop approach means that
quality is built into every step of the
issue triage and resolution process, not
bolted on afterwards. It's a powerful
way to address the triage gap.
Now, let's take a look at how this looks
in practice. We're going to watch a live
demo of an AI agent using both the core
story and the Sonar MCP servers
together to investigate, triage, and
resolve findings in a real Django
codebase. This is not a simulation. This
is the actual agent running against
actual sonar findings informed by
an actual core story instance.
Watch how fast it moves and how it makes
decisions that would require normally
deep system knowledge from a human
architect.
The agent starts by pulling the project
metrics from Sonar. 132 security
vulnerabilities. Over 1,300 security
hotspots. Nearly 2,800 total issues.
Those are real numbers from a real Sonar
analysis. And if you're looking at a
dashboard like this for a project you
just inherited or a codebase you're
evaluating for acquisition or an
application your team is about to start
building on top of, that's a lot of
findings to work through. The agent goes
straight to the blockers, 158 of them, and
immediately there's a pattern, the same
rule over and over. Revoke and change
this password. It's compromised, all
concentrated in one file in Django's
authentication test suite. Sonar is
doing exactly what you'd want it to do.
It found strings that match credential
patterns hard-coded in source code.
That's a valid important finding in
almost any context. But the question
every developer asks next is, is this
one of those times where the context
changes the answer? So the agent asks
core story, what is this file, and why
would it contain dozens of hard-coded
passwords? And the answer comes back in
seconds. This is Django's test suite for
its password hashing framework. PBKDF2,
argon 2 script. The strings sonar
flagged are test vectors. Known inputs
paired with known expected outputs that
verify the hashing algorithms produce
correct deterministic results. Now, any
experienced developer would probably
reach the same conclusion. Test files
contain test data. That part isn't
surprising, but the agent just triaged
132 findings in 10 seconds and
documented the rationale. Multiply that
across a large organization with dozens
of projects and thousands of findings,
and that's where the value compounds.
You're not waiting for a senior
developer to be available for the
obvious calls. They're handled, which
frees people up for the findings that
actually require judgment, like this
one. The agent finds a maintainability
blocker in Django's date formatting
module. The rule says methods shouldn't
differ only by capitalization, and this
class has methods named capital Y and
lowercase Y, capital D and lowercase D.
In most code bases, renaming those to
something clearer would be the right
fix. Core story explains why this one is
different. Those method names are PHP
compatible date format specifiers.
Capital Y returns a four-digit year.
Lowercase Y returns a two-digit year.
Django's template engine looks up format
characters by calling the method with
that exact name. If you rename capital
Y, every Django template that displays a
four-digit year breaks. This is a
blocker that you must never fix. Sonar
correctly identified a pattern
that's risky in general, but the actual
impact of acting on this finding is a
breaking change across the Django
ecosystem. That's not something any
static analysis tool can know. It
requires understanding the architecture,
the API contract, and how the code gets
called at runtime. This is the kind of
finding that quietly sits in a backlog
for months because no one is confident
enough to close it out. With both tools
working together, the agent resolved it
in seconds and explained exactly why.
Finally, the agent shows the other
direction, writing new code with both
tools in the loop. It asks core story
how Django handles form validation, gets
back the design patterns, writes a
validator, and submits it to sonar for
analysis. The first version comes back
with a cognitive complexity score of 68
against a limit of 15. It rewrites
with early returns, resubmits, and
passes clean. Zero issues. That's the
full cycle. Core story provides the
design context, what patterns to follow,
what the code should do. Sonar
validates the output. Does it meet your
quality and security standards, and it
all happens before a single line is
Powerful stuff. Here's what just
happened. Sonar did its job
perfectly. It found all 158 blocker
findings in the Django codebase. That's
exactly what you want. Then core story
provided essential architectural context
for every single one of those findings.
What does this code do? Who depends on
it? Why was it written this way? The
result was that all 158 findings
immediately got an automated documented
decision. We found that 132 of those
were intentional patterns that could be
resolved or ignored without direct
intervention: test vectors in Django's
password hashing framework that sonar
correctly flagged as potential
credentials. However, core story
understood that these were deliberately
weak passwords just used for testing. 25
others were deprioritized with
justification and of course the one real
critical maintainability issue was
escalated with a full blast radius
analysis: a naming convention in Django's
date formatter that would require a
breaking API change. All of this was
done in under two minutes without the
need for a senior developer to get
Now, let's talk about the ROI. Now,
these numbers are based on a typical
project that might have as many as 5,000
open findings. That's conservative for
what we'd get running Sonar in an
enterprise codebase. You can estimate
that you're recovering over 850 hours
per year per project like this. That's
one to two months or more of a senior
developer's time that you're currently
spending on this kind of triage
activity. Over $64,000 in direct cost
savings per year, assuming a $75 per hour
fully loaded developer cost. And of
course, you get the benefit of having
100% backlog coverage, meaning every
finding that we produce now gets a
documented decision with a rationale
that explains why we were able to
address this. Many of our teams are
reviewing close to 0% of our hotspot
issues. And it's because of a lack of
resources to do so. And here, of course,
we're able to resolve these with an
increased triage speed. The first
thousand findings here were all handled
in under two hours. Imagine scaling this
up. At five projects, you're looking at
over 300K per year in recovered
capacity. And that's before accounting
for the risk reduction of actually being
able to review all of our security
hotspot backlog items at scale for the
first time. There's really tremendous
gains from being able to use these two
systems together to meaningfully power
through and eliminate our backlog of
issues.
There's three big reasons that we should
all care about this. First, inside all
of our organizations, whether we like it
or not, the amount of AI generated code
is accelerating. Agents are being able
and being tasked to produce code faster
than human beings in our organizations
can review it. The only way to keep pace
is with automated context and
validation. You have to keep that
velocity from turning into a liability.
We want to ship faster and maintain the
same high level of oversight that we
need to maintain. The only way to do so
is using teams of agents around core
story and sonar. Second, code bases
are getting larger. Modern applications
are spanning hundreds of microservices
and no one person in our organization
can hold the full architecture in
their head anymore. We need the context
to be machine ready and machine
accessible. And of course, the security
stakes have never been higher. Every
potentially unaddressed vulnerability
issue or item that's identified during
analysis is potential regulatory
exposure. We need to have confidence
that we're finding and fixing the right
issues first, not just the ones that
happen to be labeled as a blocker.
We'd love to show you this combination
working on your actual codebase directly
with core story and sonar pair
programming together to resolve issues.
There are three very easy steps to get
started. You can connect both of your
MCP servers. Point your preferred AI
coding agent at both of these and
immediately start triaging. Both servers
are free to set up. There's no
migration. There's no complex
integration needed. If you want to see a
proof of concept of this system running
on your code, we'd be excited to help
you get configured. You can also sign up
directly and try it out for yourself.
Come talk to us or visit corstory.ai.
If you scan that QR code right there in
the lower right hand side of the screen,
you can open up a link that'll take you
directly to sign up for the tool. We're
excited to support you and excited to
partner with Sonar. Thanks so much.
